metadata.summary: Three-phase new-hire ramp framework (Learn/Contribute/Execute) with observable milestones anchored to real team work, not generic verbs, enabling defensible day-30, day-60, and day-90 reviews.

content.title: 30-60-90 Day Plan
content.summary: A three-phase new-hire ramp framework based on Michael Watkins's Learn / Contribute / Execute cadence. Each phase has observable milestones anchored to real team work (a release, a quota slice, a shipped doc), not generic verbs like "understand". The plan is co-authored with the hiring manager and the new hire on Day 1; plans written by HR alone become checkboxes nobody reads.

why.p: Without a structured ramp plan, new hires and managers operate on implicit, mismatched expectations. The 30-day review is the first moment misalignment surfaces — too late for easy correction. Observable artifacts per phase (PR merged, deal closed, doc published) give the manager a defensible basis for the day-30, day-60, and day-90 reviews and give the hire a clear contract for what success looks like.

list.item: New hire onboarding for any role with measurable project deliverables (engineer, sales rep, PM, marketer).
Internal transfers and promotions where role scope changes substantially.
Re-orgs where leaders need a structured first quarter against new mandates.
Drafting role-specific milestones during offer close to set explicit expectations.
Hourly or shift roles where competency comes from training scripts, not project ownership.
Sub-30-day contracts where the three-phase cadence does not fit.
Roles where outcomes depend wholly on team output — use team-level OKRs instead.
Externally mandated apprenticeship curricula (medical residency, regulated trades).

rule.statement: Every milestone in a 30-60-90 plan must be anchored to a concrete, observable artifact; never use the verbs "understand", "be familiar with", or "learn about" as milestone criteria.
rule.rationale: Vague verbs cannot be verified at the day-30 review; the manager and hire will disagree on whether the milestone is complete. Observable artifacts (PR merged, doc written, customer call completed, deal proposed) have a clear binary: done or not done.

rule.statement: Co-author the plan with the hiring manager and the new hire on Day 1; plans written by HR alone become checkboxes nobody reads after week 2.
rule.rationale: Ownership of the plan predicts adherence. When the new hire participates in setting the milestones, they internalize them as personal commitments rather than corporate requirements.

rule.statement: Days 1-30 are a learning phase; do not assign project ownership or execution pressure in the first 30 days unless the role explicitly requires immediate delivery (e.g., revenue-critical backfill).
rule.rationale: Pushing delivery into the learn phase causes early burnout, poor-quality output, and missed context that creates technical or relationship debt. The day-30 review is the correct gate for confirming readiness to contribute.

list.item: Can describe the team's current quarter goals and how this role contributes to them.
Has met all key stakeholders within and adjacent to the team.
Has read and can locate all critical documentation for the role.
Has identified one area of friction, risk, or opportunity (written 1-pager observation).
Has documented goals for days 31-60 in a shared artifact with the manager.

rule.statement: Phase 2 ends with ownership of at least one completed project, however small; a completed project is a stronger signal than partial progress on a large one.
rule.rationale: Completing a scoped project demonstrates the full delivery cycle (planning, execution, communication, handoff) at whatever scale is appropriate. A partial large project reveals nothing about completion behavior.

list.item: Has owned and completed at least one project end-to-end (scope appropriate to role and level).
Has received and acted on feedback from at least two sources (manager and peer or stakeholder).
Works with minimal supervision on familiar tasks.
Has proposed at least one improvement to a team process, tool, or practice.
Has documented goals for days 61-90 in a shared artifact.

rule.statement: By day 90, the hire must work independently on 80%+ of their responsibilities; anything below this threshold triggers a formal performance conversation, not an extension of the onboarding period.
rule.rationale: Extending the onboarding frame past 90 days without a performance conversation obscures underperformance and delays the manager's decision. The 90-day review is the formal transition from onboarding to standard performance management.

list.item: Meets or exceeds performance expectations on primary job responsibilities.
Works autonomously on 80%+ of tasks without requiring guidance.
Has contributed to team goals in a measurable, visible way.
Has a documented development plan for the next quarter.
Is integrated into team culture (included in social and professional rituals).

section.name: check-in-cadence

rule.statement: Run the day-30 review at day 25 to leave 5 days of correction opportunity before the formal milestone; never use the review as the first signal of problems.
rule.rationale: If issues surface first at the day-30 review, the correction window is zero. Running at day 25 gives 5 days to course-correct before the formal gate, converting the review from a verdict into a coaching tool.

section.p: Check-in schedule: day 7 (early pulse, informal), day 25-30 (30-day review), day 45 (skip-level: hire meets manager's manager, removes reporting-chain blind spot), day 60 (60-day review), day 90 (formal performance review).

rule.statement: Engineering 30-60-90 plans must anchor milestones to code artifacts: merged PR, deployed feature, published doc, completed code review — not "learned the codebase".
rule.rationale: Learning the codebase is a means, not a milestone. The day-30 deliverable should be a first commit, however small, that demonstrates the environment is set up and the engineer can work in the team's workflow.

example.title: Software Engineer 30-day milestone
example.note: Development environment fully set up, first small PR (bug fix or doc update) merged, all team workflows (CI/CD, code review, deployment) completed at least once, key stakeholders introduced.

example.title: Software Engineer 60-day milestone
example.note: Owns and delivers a scoped feature independently — from technical design review through merge and monitoring for one week post-deploy. Participates in sprint planning and owns at least 2 code reviews per sprint.

example.title: Software Engineer 90-day milestone
example.note: Recognized as a productive team member by the team (peer feedback). Owns a medium-complexity feature. Has contributed to at least one architectural discussion with a documented position. Leads or presents in one team sync.

rule.statement: Sales 30-60-90 plans must anchor to pipeline metrics, not activity counts; "made 50 calls" is not a milestone — "built a qualified pipeline of $X" is.
rule.rationale: Activity counts measure effort, not output. A sales representative can make 100 calls to unqualified prospects and have zero qualified pipeline. Pipeline value and deal stage distribution are the leading indicators of ramp performance.

example.title: Sales Representative 30-day milestone
example.note: Product certified (passed internal product test), completed 5 ride-along calls with senior reps, built first territory plan, run first 3 discovery calls independently.

example.title: Sales Representative 60-day milestone
example.note: 50% of full-quota pipeline built in the CRM with stage-correct opportunities. Has presented at least 5 demos. Has submitted at least 2 proposals. Has developed a territory account map.

example.title: Sales Representative 90-day milestone
example.note: Hit ramp quota (50-75% of full quota). Has closed at least 1 deal independently. Manages full sales cycle without manager shadowing. Has a territory strategy for Q2 documented.

antipattern.description: Using the same plan template for engineering and sales roles without role-specific adaptation.
antipattern.reason: A Sales 60-day milestone (50% pipeline) is fundamentally unlike an engineering one (feature delivered). Generic templates produce milestones that don't match either role's actual delivery cycle, making the review a formality.

antipattern.description: Setting stretch goals in the Learn phase (days 1-30) to signal high expectations.
antipattern.reason: Stretch goals in the first 30 days reward speed over quality, incentivize the hire to skip context-building, and produce poor output that creates downstream debt. The manager signals expectations through the day-60 and day-90 milestones, not by front-loading delivery.

antipattern.description: Plans written solely by HR using a generic template, then handed to the hire on Day 1 as a done document.
antipattern.reason: Plans the hire did not co-author become external checklists with no ownership. Drift starts in week 2; by day 30, nobody references them. Thirty minutes of co-authoring on Day 1 produces a plan the hire treats as a personal commitment.

template.purpose: Bash scaffold: given a role slug and JD file, generates a markdown plan skeleton with the three phases.
